guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, generators copy-paste a volume float and forget to scale for pace. Match-3 at .punchy is too loud; arcade at .subtle disappears. Presets capture the calibration. Mirrors the AnimationIntensity pattern intentionally so generators learn one mental model.
examples: RIGHT: SoundEngine.shared.intensity = .standard  // SoundIntensity: standard (match-3)
RIGHT (override): var i = SoundIntensity.standard; i.sfxVolume = 0.5; i.musicVolume = 0.25; SoundEngine.shared.intensity = i
WRONG: hardcoded `sound.play(.match3Pop, volume: 0.7)` everywhere — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Background music tracks MUST fade in (1.0–1.5s) and fade out (1.0s) — NEVER hard-cut. Hard cuts are jarring and feel like a glitch. SoundEngine.loop(_:fadeIn:) handles this: `sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5)`. Track switches: stopMusic(fadeOut: 0.3) THEN loop(.next, fadeIn: 1.0) — for a clean crossfade-via-stop. App-quit / sheet-dismiss / scene-change: stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) before transition. SFX are exempt — they're already short, fade is built into the sound itself.
guidelines.why: Universal audio production rule. Hard cuts on long-form audio = listener notices the seam = breaks immersion. Filed 2026-05-04 with SoundEngine ship — the engine enforces fade defaults so hard cuts only happen if a generator explicitly passes 0.
examples: RIGHT: sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5) on game start; sound.stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) on game-over screen.
WRONG: musicPlayer.play() / musicPlayer.stop() without fade. Listener hears the abrupt cut.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games (match-3, arcade-with-waves, puzzles-with-difficulty-tiers, runners, idle clickers, platformers with progressing stages) MUST use the `effects.level-manager` Lego component instead of inventing target-score / progression math from scratch. LevelManager provides: difficulty curves (.casual 1.5× / .standard 1.4× / .hardcore 1.3× / .accelerating / .custom), per-level moves budget, suggestedPaletteSize / suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier / suggestedAnimationIntensity helpers, persistent currentLevel via UserDefaults, and a celebration window (`celebratingLevelUp` / `celebrationProgress`) for the banner + SFX. Wire it as `@StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "<game>", baseTarget: <num>, baseMovesAllowed: <num>, curve: .standard)`.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager ship. Without a shared component, every progression game writes ad-hoc 'targetScore *= 1.5' arithmetic, forgets to persist current level, and has no celebration moment — users finish level 1, see the same screen, and lose engagement. LevelManager + the celebration window turn 'beat target' into a reward.
examples: RIGHT: @StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "match3", baseTarget: 1500, baseMovesAllowed: 30, curve: .standard); HUD reads `Text("LEVEL \(levels.currentLevel)")` and `Text("\(levels.currentTarget)")`. On target hit: `levels.advance()`.
RIGHT (UI): scaleEffect on level pill = `1.0 + levels.celebrationProgress * 0.20`; level-up banner opacity = `Double(levels.celebrationProgress)`.
RIGHT (palette growth): `engine.symbols = Array(["A","B","C","D","E","F","G","H"].prefix(levels.suggestedPaletteSize))` — palette starts at 5 colors, grows by +1 every 5 levels, capped at 8.
WRONG: hardcoded `private let targetScore = 1500`, no progression. User beats it, sees nothing change, exits app, never returns.
WRONG: ad-hoc `if score >= 1500 { score = 0; targetScore = 2500 }` — no curve, no persistence, no celebration.

guidelines.rule: When a player advances a level, the UI MUST acknowledge it with a 1-3 second celebration: (a) a prominent 'LEVEL N' banner that inflates and fades, (b) a level-up SFX (SoundEngine.play(.levelUp) — pitch-shift +2 semitones for stronger effect), (c) a brief scale/glow pulse on the level pill / score readout, (d) a tactile pause (~0.5–1s) before the new board / wave is presented. Skipping the celebration means the player isn't told they accomplished something — and the dopamine hit of progression is the entire reason to play. LevelManager provides `celebratingLevelUp: Bool` and `celebrationProgress: CGFloat` (0→1→0 across the window) — bind animations to these.
guidelines.why: Universal game-design principle. Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Without celebration, a level-up feels like a level-down — the user hits the target and the game just changes the number. With celebration (banner + SFX + pulse), the user FEELS the win. Match3 v8 implementation: level pill scaleEffect(1.0 + celebrationProgress * 0.20), banner Text scaleEffect(0.7 + celebrationProgress * 0.6) + opacity(celebrationProgress), levelUp SFX with +2 pitch.
examples: RIGHT: levels.advance() triggers celebrationProgress ramp 0→1 over 0.5s, plateau 0.5s, 1→0 over 2s. View binds banner opacity/scale to celebrationProgress; SFX plays at the start.
RIGHT (audio sequence): on target hit → sound.play(.victory) → 450ms pause → sound.play(.levelUp, pitchSemitones: 2) → levels.advance() triggers banner.
WRONG: levels.advance() called silently with no UI reaction. Player isn't sure if anything happened.
WRONG: instant snap-cut to next level (no banner, no SFX, no delay). Feels like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: When a player fails a level (out of moves, lives depleted, time up), the game MUST allow replay of the SAME level — never auto-demote to a lower level or back to L1. Save the player's progression at all times. RESTART buttons should reset score/moves WITHIN the current level, not bump levels.currentLevel down. Players who hit a hard level will replay it many times; demoting them would be a punishment that destroys engagement. The only way to drop a level should be a deliberate user action (e.g. a 'Reset Progress' settings toggle, never the in-game RESTART button).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager. Hardcoded into LevelManager API: `replayCurrentLevel()` is a no-op on persistence; only `reset()` (explicit reset-progress action) resets currentLevel to 1. Universal UX rule — applied incorrectly, players quit the game forever after one rage-fail.
examples: RIGHT: RESTART button rebuilds engine, refreshes moves to levels.currentMovesAllowed, but does NOT touch levels.currentLevel.
RIGHT: 'Reset Progress' in settings menu calls levels.reset() — opt-in only.
WRONG: RESTART resets currentLevel to 1. Player who beat 5 levels and rage-quit on L6 has to redo all 5.

guidelines.rule: Higher levels should feel different across MULTIPLE dimensions, not just 'higher target number.' For tile/match games: grow the symbol palette (LevelManager.suggestedPaletteSize: 5→6 at L5, 7 at L10, 8 at L15), shrink moves budget if pacing demands it (.accelerating curve), bump animation intensity (LevelManager.suggestedAnimationIntensity: 'subtle' L1-4 → 'standard' L5-9 → 'punchy' L10+). For arcade games: spawn-rate (LevelManager.suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier: 1.0× → 3.0× capped at L20+), enemy variety, projectile speed, intermittent boss waves at L5/L10/L15. The TARGET getting larger is just one knob — alone, it produces 'same game, takes longer.' Multi-knob progression produces 'this level FEELS different.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Real Candy Crush / Bejeweled / etc. grow many things per level (board shape, special-tile generation rate, blockers, mission types). Reference apps in the catalog should at minimum grow palette + intensity for tile games, spawn-rate + intensity for arcade games. LevelManager exposes the suggested helpers so generators don't have to invent them.
examples: RIGHT (match-3): L1: 5 colors, .standard intensity. L5: 6 colors, .standard. L10: 7 colors, .punchy. L15: 8 colors, .punchy. Each level feels harder for a NEW reason.
RIGHT (arcade): L1: 2 enemies, slow projectiles, .standard intensity. L5: 4 enemies, faster projectiles, .standard. L10: 6 enemies + occasional bomb-spawn, .punchy.
WRONG: every level identical except target=basicTarget*1.4^N. Player perceives 'same game just slower to finish' — no surprise, no novelty, drops off.

guidelines.rule: Choose LevelCurve by intended play session length: `.casual` (1.5× per level, fast ramp) for short kid-friendly sessions where players should reach high levels in 10-15 minutes. `.standard` (1.4×) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper) where 30-60 min sessions are typical. `.hardcore` (1.3×, slow ramp) for endurance/idle/score-attack games designed for long-term engagement. `.accelerating` (gentle 1.3× early, climbs to 1.6× by L20+) for games that should onboard easily but become a real challenge — typical for puzzle franchises. `.custom` for games with non-monotonic difficulty (boss levels, breather levels, jumps).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Same generator-mental-model pattern as AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity — pick a preset by game pace, not by inventing arithmetic. Wrong curve = engagement disaster: hardcore curve on a casual game = players quit at L4; casual curve on hardcore = players hit L99 in an hour and have nothing left.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "kid-game", curve: .casual) — fast progression, frequent level-up celebrations.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "match3", curve: .standard) — balanced.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "score-attack", curve: .hardcore) — slow ramp.
WRONG: pick a curve based on coin flip / didn't think about pace; ship a casual-curve match-3 (player ceiling-hits at L20) or hardcore-curve casual game (player gives up at L4).

guidelines.rule: Persist `currentLevel` (the structural progression) via UserDefaults. Do NOT persist `currentScore` (the within-level progress) — it should reset on app launch. Players don't expect to resume a partially-played level mid-game; they expect to come back, see their level number, and start that level fresh. LevelManager handles the level persistence under key 'LevelManager.<gameID>.currentLevel'. SoundEngine.muted is also persisted (separately). Anything else (current score, board state, current cascade depth, hint pair) is ephemeral.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager. Saving mid-level state is high-effort + low-value: requires Codable engine state, can desync if engine impl changes, players have to mentally context-switch back into a half-finished puzzle. Saving level-only is what users expect from casual-game UX.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager persists currentLevel automatically. App launches → onAppear sets engine.symbols based on suggestedPaletteSize, moves = currentMovesAllowed, status = 'Level N — target M'. Score starts at 0.
WRONG: persist current Match3Engine state across launches. Brittle, annoying for users, breaks when engine model evolves.

guidelines.rule: Personal best / high-score should be surfaced in the GAME-OVER (or run-summary) panel, NOT in the always-visible in-play HUD. During active play, in-play high-score chrome (BEST pills, live 'X to beat' / '+N ahead' deltas) is visual noise that distracts from the actual gameplay — players are mid-decision and don't want a constant comparison ticker. The end-of-run panel is when the comparison is meaningful: it's a single moment, the player has earned their score, and seeing 'Best: 12,450' alongside 'Your run: 11,200' creates the natural motivation to play again. Use HighScoreManager.commit(score:) at game-over → it returns true on a new best, fires celebrationProgress for a 'NEW BEST!' flourish on the panel. The in-play HUD shows ONLY the active counters (score / level / moves / time).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Initial implementation v9 put BEST + live delta + 'NEW BEST!' chrome in the always-visible HUD. User feedback: 'why did you go so overboard with the highscore thing? this is a super ugly header. have you seen a high score on a game, it shouldn't show up until the game is done so the user doesn't get so distracted.' Established games (Candy Crush, Bejeweled, Threes, every console arcade game) put high score in the game-over screen — never as constant in-play chrome. v9.1 moved it to a modal that appears only at moves==0.
examples: RIGHT: in-play HUD = SCORE | LEVEL | MOVES (just active counters). Game-over panel = OUT OF MOVES / NEW BEST! header, big run-total readout, 'Best: 12,450' line with trophy icon, PLAY AGAIN button.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(totalScore) at moves==0 OR game-end → returns true on new best → panel header flips to 'NEW BEST!' with gold styling.
WRONG: BEST pill always visible in the HUD with live 'N to beat' / '+N ahead' delta during play. Distracts from the gameplay decision.
WRONG: 'NEW BEST!' celebration that fires DURING play (e.g. mid-cascade). Save it for the game-over moment so it's a clean reward.

guidelines.rule: Multi-level games MUST track BOTH a cumulative score (carries across levels in the session) AND a per-level score (zeros each level for progress-toward-target). Surface BOTH in the HUD: TOTAL = cumulative session score (this is what you compare against personal best); LEVEL SCORE = this level's progress, drives the level-progress bar / star thresholds / level-up trigger. Wiring: `totalScore = baseTotalForLevel + engine.score`. On level-up: `baseTotalForLevel = totalScore` (anchor the new level's floor), then re-seed engine (engine.score back to 0). Without two-tier scores, players who beat 5 levels see 'their score' reset on level transitions and lose the sense of session progress.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user feedback 'after going to a new level my score gets reset to 0 instead of setting some additional point goal.' Same conceptual error as level-replay-not-demote — UX should never feel like progress was wiped. Match-3 / RPG / arcade-with-waves / endless-mode all benefit. Two-score model is also what HighScoreManager.commit(score:) should be called with — the cumulative TOTAL, not the per-level score.
examples: RIGHT: HUD shows TOTAL: 12,450 prominently; LEVEL SCORE: 320 / 3,000 below it. On level-up, TOTAL stays at 12,450 + earnings; LEVEL SCORE flips to 0 / 4,200 for the new level.
RIGHT: highScores.commit(score: totalScore) — commits cumulative session total, not per-level.
WRONG: only show engine.score, which resets every level. User thinks they're starting over each time.
WRONG: commit per-level score to HighScoreManager — your 'best' becomes 'highest single level' instead of 'longest run.'

guidelines.rule: Mutable in-game counters (moves remaining, lives, ammo, time) MUST be guarded by `max(0, …)` and gated at their entry points. A `moves -= 1` followed by a refund-on-fail pattern can race or compound — multiple events firing close together can push the counter negative. Pattern: `guard moves > 0 else { /* show out-of-moves UI */; return }` BEFORE the action, then `moves = max(0, moves - 1)` only after the action commits successfully. Failed actions (bad swap / missed shot / etc.) should NOT charge the counter — players punished for mis-timing input churn out fast.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after user reported 'somehow I managed to get to -1 moves' in catalog Match3 v8. Bug was: `moves -= 1` ran before the swap-success check; refund only happened on the mis-input path. Fixed with explicit guard at entry + max(0,…) on the decrement + don't-charge-bad-swaps. Universal pattern for any decrementing counter.
examples: RIGHT: guard moves > 0 else { status = "Out of moves — RESTART to retry"; sound.play(.uiError); return }; ... if !didSwap { sound.play(.uiError); return /* don't charge */ }; moves = max(0, moves - 1)
WRONG: moves -= 1; if !didSwap { moves += 1; return }  — racy under rapid input, can bottom out below 0.
WRONG: charge a move on failed actions. Player who mis-drags loses count for nothing.

guidelines.rule: Game engines that run `resolveCascades()` / equivalent setup logic during init MUST explicitly zero the score at the end of init. Initial-fill cascades produce score increments that are NOT user-earned. Without explicit zeroing, fresh games + level-up board re-seeds start at 200-1000+ ghost score depending on seed luck. Pattern in init: `_ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0`. Engine.swap() / engine.advance() — the user-driven entry points — are the only paths that should award score.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from real bug in catalog Match3Engine. init() called resolveCascades() to clear initial-fill matches before showing the board, which awarded score for each cascade. Only the event log was cleared, not the score. User report: 'my score never starts at 0.' Same pattern applies to Tetris (initial cleared rows during piece-spawn-warmup), RPG (XP from seed-creation enemies), any game with auto-resolve in init.
examples: RIGHT: init() { ... refillAll(); _ = resolveCascades(); lastResolvedMatches = []; score = 0 }
WRONG: init() runs resolveCascades() (which awards score), then only clears event log — fresh games start at 200-1000 'ghost' points.

guidelines.rule: Score readouts ≥1000 MUST use thousands separators ('12,450' not '12450'; '1,234,567' not '1234567'). Numeric readability drops sharply past 4 digits without separators. HighScoreManager.formatNumber(_:) provides the canonical formatter (uses NumberFormatter with .decimal style); use it for ALL score-like numbers (current score, best score, target, total, delta). Targets that are still 4-digit (like '1500') can be unformatted but consistency is better — format everything once you cross the threshold.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with HighScoreManager ship. Universal readability rule. '125000' is a wall of digits; '125,000' is instantly parseable. Costs nothing, consistency is high-value.
examples: RIGHT: Text(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(totalScore)) → '12,450'
RIGHT: Text("+\(HighScoreManager.formatNumber(delta)) ahead") → '+1,200 ahead'
WRONG: Text("\(totalScore)") → '12450' (no separator)

guidelines.rule: Game state transitions (level-up, game-over, round-restart, board reseed) MUST be SYNCHRONOUS and ATOMIC. Do NOT split state mutation across `Task { try await Task.sleep(...) }` segments where the visual celebration runs in parallel with state-resetting code. While the user waits for sleeps to complete, they can swap / fire / tap, and the next render-driven turn reads HALF-MUTATED state — causing a cluster of bugs: score resets to 0 (new turn reads stale baseTotalForLevel), counters don't refresh (UI bound to var that hasn't been reassigned yet), RESTART takes you to the wrong level (level-number advanced but engine hasn't been swapped). The fix: do all state mutation in ONE turn (typically inside one `withAnimation` block), and run the celebration banner via a parallel @Published value (e.g. LevelManager.celebrationProgress) that the View animates from independently. SFX is fine to fire as a delayed Task — audio-only, no state mutation, can't race.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Match3 v9 → v9.2 incident. v9 split level-up across two `try await Task.sleep` segments (450ms + 650ms) with state-resets happening AFTER the sleeps. User reported three distinct bugs that were all the same race: 'my score went back to 0 at a certain level' (next cascade read stale baseTotalForLevel), 'moves didn't reset after some levels' (UI bound to moves var that hadn't been reassigned yet), 'RESTART only takes me back to the previous level' (RESTART read half-mutated state — level number advanced but engine not yet swapped). v9.2 made the entire transition atomic in one withAnimation block; banner animation runs from levels.celebrationProgress which advances on its own.
examples: RIGHT (atomic): when level target hit → run all in one turn: `levels.advance(); engine = newEngine; moves = levels.currentMovesAllowed; baseTotalForLevel = totalScore; victoryAnnounced = false`. The banner animates from levels.celebrationProgress (a @Published 0→1→0 value the controller advances internally). SFX fires as a one-shot Task — audio only.

RIGHT (visual delay decoupled from state): celebration banner reads `if levels.celebratingLevelUp { ... .opacity(Double(levels.celebrationProgress)) }`. Banner can take 3 seconds to fade out — gameplay state is already consistent at frame 1.
WRONG: `Task { try await Task.sleep(450ms); levels.advance(); try await Task.sleep(650ms); engine = newEngine; moves = newMoves }`. During the sleeps, user input lands on stale state.

WRONG: setting `victoryAnnounced = false` inside an async withAnimation that runs LATER than other state mutations — gives the next turn's runCascadeLoop() permission to re-fire the win condition before the engine has been swapped.
Acceptable async (audio only): `Task { try await Task.sleep(350ms); sound.play(.levelUp) }` — no state mutation, no race possible.

guidelines.rule: Enemy / bot / NPC AI in arcade-action games MUST NOT aim with laser-perfect accuracy at the player. Add aim-jitter (±15-30° of randomness on each shot, kind-dependent) so enemies miss often enough to be enjoyable, not punishing. Pattern: when firing, compute the ideal aim angle to the player, then perturb it by `Double.random(in: -jitter...jitter)` where jitter is a per-enemy-kind constant (e.g. casual saucer = 0.45 rad ≈ ±25°, aggressive hunter = 0.18 rad ≈ ±10°, boss = 0.30 rad). Same principle for enemy MOVEMENT: don't beeline toward the player — drift toward periodic waypoints that orbit the player's position with random offset, refreshed every 1-3 seconds. Without jitter, the enemy plays a perfect game theorist; the player feels like the dice are loaded against them and quits. With kind-tuned jitter, the player can dodge skillfully and feels rewarded.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with the Asteroids enemy-ship rollout. The natural instinct in implementation is `aim = atan2(player.y - enemy.y, player.x - enemy.x)` and fire — produces a frustrating game where the player can never escape. Real arcade enemies (Galaga, Asteroids original, Geometry Wars, even Space Invaders' descend pattern) all build in randomness so the player has agency. Tuned per-enemy jitter is also how you create archetypal threats (drone < soldier < elite) without coding entirely separate behavior.
examples: RIGHT (per-kind table): enum EnemyKind { var aimJitter: Double { switch self { case .saucer: 0.45; case .hunter: 0.18; case .boss: 0.30 } } } — wider jitter for casual enemies, narrower for elite.
RIGHT (movement): enemies pick a random waypoint near the player every ~2.5s, steer toward it. Hunters refresh every ~1s for tighter pursuit; bosses every ~3s for slower drift.
RIGHT (per-shot jitter): aimAngle += Double.random(in: -jitter...jitter); fire shot at aimAngle.
WRONG: aimAngle = atan2(player.y - enemy.y, player.x - enemy.x); fire — perfect-aim sniper enemies. Player gets shot the instant they're visible.
WRONG: enemy.position = lerp(enemy.position, player.position, 0.1) — enemies path-find directly to player. Player can't outrun them, ever.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games SHOULD include boss encounters every 5-10 levels (LevelManager.isBossLevel + LevelManager.bossLevelInterval = 5 by default). A boss encounter has: (a) distinct entry messaging — 'BOSS WAVE' toast in red, distinct SFX (arcadePowerUp / level-up chord), (b) a multi-hit boss enemy with visible health indicator (pip bar above the enemy), (c) higher score reward (3-5× a regular enemy), (d) wave/level advance is GATED on the boss being dead, not just the regular enemies, (e) a satisfying death payoff — the boss spawns 2-3 minor enemies on death so the player has to clean up (creates a 'and another thing!' moment). Skipping boss waves makes progression feel monotonous; level 7 looks just like level 6 just slightly faster. Boss waves are the rhythm-marker that breaks up the gameplay loop.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside the Asteroids boss/mothership pattern. User said: 'it's good to have a boss level or something like that occasionally.' Universal arcade/RPG/roguelike pattern — every game with progression that engages players for >30 minutes uses boss-rhythm to break monotony. LevelManager exposes isBossLevel as the cadence helper so generators don't have to invent the math.
examples: RIGHT (engine): if levels.isBossLevel { spawnMothership() } else { spawnRegularEnemies() }. Wave-clear gate: rocks.isEmpty && !enemies.contains(where: { $0.kind == .boss })
RIGHT (visual): boss has distinct shape (hex disc vs saucer/triangle) + health pips above its body + entry banner in different color (red vs blue for regular waves)
RIGHT (death payoff): on boss death, spawn 2-3 minor enemies near the boss position. score += 1000 (vs 250 for a regular enemy). Player cleans them up before wave advance.
WRONG: every level identical. Wave 7 looks like wave 6 but spawns 1 more rock. Player perceives 'just slower to finish.'
WRONG: boss uses the same model + behavior as regular enemies. No visual cue of threat level.

guidelines.rule: Don't ship a game with one enemy archetype that scales with level. Define an `EnemyKind` enum with at least 2-3 archetypes (casual / aggressive / elite or boss), each with a per-kind table of behavior constants: radius, moveSpeed, fireInterval, aimJitter, pointValue, maxHits. The game spawns DIFFERENT KIND mixes per wave/level (e.g. wave 3 = saucers only, wave 6 = saucers + 1 hunter, wave 5 = boss). This produces visually + mechanically distinct experiences that the player learns to read at a glance — 'oh a hunter, I need to dodge faster' vs 'a saucer, I can take my time.' Implementation pattern: `enum EnemyKind { var radius: Double { switch self {...} }; var moveSpeed: Double { ... }; var fireInterval: Double { ... }; var aimJitter: Double { ... }; var pointValue: Int { ... }; var maxHits: Int { ... } }` — all behavior comes from the kind, no per-instance flags scattered through the engine.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. User feedback after first Asteroids enemy pass: 'diversity of enemies and levels is good.' Universal arcade-action pattern — Galaga's bosses + escorts + drones, Asteroids original's small/medium/large rocks + saucers, Space Invaders' three alien rows. The kind-table approach makes adding a new enemy archetype a single enum case + lookup-table extension, no engine refactor. Mirrors how AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity work — preset by kind, not per-instance tuning.
examples: RIGHT (Asteroids v3): enum AsteroidsEnemyKind { case saucer, hunter, mothership } with computed properties for radius/moveSpeed/fireInterval/aimJitter/pointValue/maxHits. Different waves spawn different mixes.
RIGHT (Space Invaders pattern): enum InvaderKind { case octopus, crab, squid, ufo } each with score / fire-pattern / row-position.
WRONG: one Enemy struct with a `level: Int` field and behavior switching on level — produces 'fast-saucer' rather than 'hunter,' visually identical.

guidelines.rule: When a game engine produces transient events that the View needs to react to (paddle-hit, enemy-killed, ball-bounced, level-cleared, etc.), expose them via a `lastEvent` property + `mutating func consumeLastEvent() -> EventEnum?` rather than making the View reverse-engineer events from state diffs. Pattern: engine sets `lastEvent = .paddleHit` inside the relevant private function; the View calls `if let event = engine.consumeLastEvent() { switch event { ... } }` once per tick. Single-shot consumption (read clears it) ensures SFX fire exactly once per event. Without this pattern, the View has to diff `engine.score` / `engine.lives` / `engine.rocks.count` between frames and infer 'wait, that 50-point bump means a small rock died' — brittle, false-positives on every score change, breaks if the engine adds new score sources.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from PongEngine + AsteroidsEngine event handling. PongEngine's `lastEvent: PongEvent?` + `consumeLastEvent()` is the canonical implementation — paddle-hit, wall-bounce, leftScored, rightScored. Earlier Asteroids implementation tried to detect wave-clear by 'rocks count grew between ticks' and false-fired on every rock split (50 points = small split? or wave-clear-bonus 50? unrecoverable without additional state). Engine-as-event-source is much cleaner.
examples: RIGHT: enum PongEvent { case paddleHit, wallBounce, leftScored, rightScored }; engine has `private(set) var lastEvent: PongEvent?` + `mutating func consumeLastEvent() -> PongEvent? { let e = lastEvent; lastEvent = nil; return e }`. View: `if let e = engine.consumeLastEvent() { switch e { case .paddleHit: sound.play(.match3Pop) } }`
WRONG: View tracks `lastObservedScore` and tries to deduce 'oh, score jumped 500 = wave clear' — fails when other score sources exist (achievements, bonuses, multipliers)
WRONG: engine fires SFX directly via @ObservableObject hooks — couples engine to audio framework, breaks unit tests, breaks Codable round-trips.

guidelines.rule: Enemies that take more than one hit MUST have a visible health indicator. The simplest pattern: a row of `maxHits` small pips (filled rectangles) above the enemy body, where `hitsRemaining` of them are filled (yellow/gold) and the rest are empty (white at low opacity). Without a visible health bar, the player can't tell why their shots aren't killing the enemy — they assume they're missing or that the game is broken. The pip count communicates 'this is a tougher enemy' AND 'you're making progress.' For very-many-hit bosses (>10 hits), use a continuous bar instead of discrete pips. Layout: 14-18px above the enemy body, total width = 1.6× enemy radius.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Asteroids mothership pattern (maxHits = 5). Universal arcade rule. Galaga capital ships, R-Type bosses, Geometry Wars elite enemies — every multi-hit enemy has a health indicator. Without it the player thinks the enemy is invincible, gets frustrated, quits. Pip count also tunes engagement: 3 pips = 'tougher' / 5 pips = 'a fight' / 10+ pips = 'use a continuous bar instead.'
examples: RIGHT (3-5 hits): row of N small filled rectangles, `hitsRemaining` of them gold, rest at low-opacity white. 14px above enemy.
RIGHT (>10 hits): continuous bar with `hitsRemaining / maxHits` width filled.
WRONG: no visual indicator — player shoots boss 4 times, sees no change, concludes 'this enemy is broken' or 'I'm missing.'

guidelines.rule: Boss visual must be distinctly colored from regular enemies. Players need to read 'this is the boss' at a glance — not by examining the health bar or counting hit-points. Use a different shape (regular = saucer; boss = large hex), a different palette (regular = blue/cyan; boss = magenta/pink/red — 'warning' colors), and a noticeably larger radius (1.5-2.5× regular). Same for the boss-wave entry toast — different color than the regular wave-up toast. The visual hierarchy communicates threat level before the player reads any text.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with Asteroids mothership ship. Universal game-design rule rooted in colorblind-friendly threat-signaling: red/magenta = elite/boss, blue/cyan = regular, green = friendly/health. The shape distinction also handles colorblind cases (a hex looks different from a saucer regardless of palette).
examples: RIGHT: regular saucer = cyan oval, hunter = red triangle, mothership = pink/magenta hex. Boss-wave toast = red. Regular wave-up toast = blue.
WRONG: same color/shape for boss as regular, just bigger. Boss reads as 'big saucer,' not 'BOSS.'

guidelines.rule: Grid-based pursuit games (Pacman, dungeon crawlers, top-down chase games) MUST give enemies DIFFERENT AI personalities, not all-random direction picking. Define a `Personality` enum on the actor with at least 3-4 archetypes: `.chaser` (minimize Manhattan distance to player), `.ambusher` (target the tile 4 squares ahead of the player in the player's current direction), `.patrol` (chase when far/scatter when close — usually distance > 5 = chase, ≤ 5 = random), `.random` (legacy walkable-pick). Implementation pattern: `func pickEnemyDirection(for actor: Actor, hero: Actor?) -> Direction` returns a personality-specific choice from the walkable-direction set. Frightened/power-pellet mode flips ALL personalities to flee-from-hero (maximize Manhattan distance). Without personality differentiation, 4 ghosts feel like 'a swarm with random noise' instead of 'four distinct threats with reading-able patterns.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with the Pacman v2 personality rollout. The original Pacman ghost AI is iconic precisely BECAUSE the four ghosts have different personalities — Blinky (chase), Pinky (ambush), Inky (vector), Clyde (patrol). Players unconsciously learn to read each ghost's pattern and play around them. Uniform random picks make the gameplay 'noisy random' rather than 'pattern-based.' Generalizes to any pursuit game on a grid — dungeon crawlers, hide-and-seek, top-down chase games. Different from arcade-action's jitter rule (game-bot-ai-jitter-not-laser-accurate), which applies to free-2D AI with continuous aim.
examples: RIGHT (Pacman): enum Personality { case chaser, ambusher, patrol, random }; on each enemy tick, pickEnemyDirection() switches on personality. Blinky = chaser, Pinky = ambusher, Clyde = patrol, Sue = random.
RIGHT (ambusher target projection): `let target = projectedTarget(from: hero.position, direction: hero.direction, distance: 4)`; pick walkable that minimizes Manhattan to target.
RIGHT (frightened override): all personalities flip to maximize-distance-from-hero when actor.frightenedTicks > 0.
WRONG: all ghosts use the same `pickGhostDirection()` random walkable. They feel like a swarm — players don't develop the 'oh, the orange one is dumb so I can run past it' intuition.

guidelines.rule: Tower-defense / wave-based games SHOULD inject a DIFFERENT enemy mix on boss waves, not just 'same composition + 1 boss enemy.' Pattern: when `LevelManager.isBossLevel` is true, call `engine.startWave(composition:)` with a heavy-tank-dominated mix (e.g. 5 normal + 6 heavy + 2 fast) instead of the default `startWave(count:)` that uses the engine's progressive default. Heavy-dominated boss waves require fundamentally different tower placement strategy (more cannons / fewer fast-tracking) — that's what makes the boss wave feel special vs 'wave 5 has 1 extra enemy.' Pair with a distinct entry SFX (arcadePowerUp) and a 'BOSS WAVE' label in status text. Without composition injection, the boss-wave concept reduces to a number on a screen.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with TowerDefense v2 polish. TowerDefenseEngine already had `startWave(composition: [(EnemyKind, Int)])` as a public API but no example used it for boss-flavored waves. The View now invokes the composition variant on every 5th wave. Generalizes to any wave-based game with multiple enemy types — Plants vs Zombies (lots of conehead zombies on round 5), Bloons (more lead bloons on the round-5 boss), Kingdom Rush, etc.
examples: RIGHT: if levels.isBossLevel { engine.startWave(composition: [(.normal, 5), (.heavy, 6), (.fast, 2)]) } else { engine.startWave(count: 7 + engine.wave) }
RIGHT (visual): boss-wave status text reads 'BOSS WAVE 5 — heavy assault incoming' and the entry SFX is arcadePowerUp instead of uiButtonTap.
WRONG: boss wave = same composition + 1 extra enemy. Players feel no strategic difference.

guidelines.rule: When wiring SFX to a game-engine tick (Asteroids/Pong/Pacman/Frogger/TowerDefense pattern), use the pre/post snapshot model: capture `preLives = engine.lives` / `preScore = engine.score` / `preEnemyCount = engine.enemies.count` BEFORE calling `engine.update()`, then compare against post-update values. Diff > 0 = an event occurred this tick → fire the appropriate SFX. This is the View-side complement to the engine `lastEvent` / `consumeLastEvent()` pattern (game-engine-event-consumer-pattern) — pre/post snapshot is for engines that don't expose discrete events but DO have observable scalar state (score, lives, enemy count). Critical: do this BEFORE `if engine.phase == .playing` checks since some events (game-over, win) are themselves the phase change.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Pacman/Frogger/TD v2 wiring. Earlier ContentView code had `engine.update()` followed by status text mutation, with no SFX hook. The pre/post snapshot pattern surfaces all discrete events the engine produces without requiring an `Event` enum. Tower kills + base breaches in TD: postEnemyCount < preEnemyCount tells you something disappeared; livesLost = preLives - postLives. Tower kills = (enemies that disappeared) - (enemies that reached the base). Composable, debounce-friendly, and works across any tick-based game.
examples: RIGHT: let preLives = engine.lives; let preEnemyCount = engine.enemies.count; engine.update(dt: dt); let livesLost = preLives - engine.lives; let killedThisTick = preEnemyCount - engine.enemies.count; let towerKills = max(0, killedThisTick - livesLost); if towerKills > 0 { sound.play(.arcadeExplosion) }; if livesLost > 0 { sound.play(.uiError) }

RIGHT (Pacman): scoreDelta = engine.score - scoreBefore; if scoreDelta >= 200 { sound.play(.match3Burst) /* ghost eaten */ } else if scoreDelta >= 100 { sound.play(.victory, pitchSemitones: 4) /* fruit */ } else if scoreDelta >= 50 { sound.play(.arcadePowerUp) /* power pellet */ } else if scoreDelta >= 10 { sound.play(.match3Pop) /* pellet */ }
WRONG: trying to detect events by polling state every render frame — you can't tell *which tick* a delta happened on, debounce becomes impossible.

guidelines.rule: Reference apps that share a game engine (e.g. Pacman + Frogger both using `GridAdventureEngine`) MUST configure the engine via STATIC FACTORY METHODS that return distinct configurations, NOT by hardcoded conditionals inside the engine. Pattern: `static func froggerLaneMap() -> Engine` and `static func pacmanArcadeMap() -> Engine` — each returns a fully-configured engine with the appropriate map, actor roster, and per-actor properties (e.g. ghost personalities). The engine itself stays game-agnostic — it doesn't know whether it's Pacman or Frogger. View-level differences (cell size, color palette, status text) live in the View. This separation lets the same engine power 5+ reference apps with different feels without engine bloat.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with Pacman/Frogger v2. GridAdventureEngine has factory methods for adventure-castle / frogger-lane / maze-pellet / pacman-arcade maps. Each returns a distinct engine state. Without this pattern, a single ContentView would have to construct + populate the engine inline (200+ lines of map data per game) and any engine API changes would break all factories silently. With factory methods, the engine evolves cleanly and new games are just new factories.
examples: RIGHT: GridAdventureEngine.pacmanArcadeMap() returns engine with maze + 4 ghosts (each with personality), pellets, fruit, power pellets. froggerLaneMap() returns same engine type with road/water tiles + vehicle/log actors.
WRONG: GridAdventureEngine has `var gameMode: GameMode` field and switches behavior on it. Engine becomes a god object that handles every game type.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid games (Pacman, Frogger, dungeon crawlers, Snake) on macOS MUST step the game logic at 6-10 tiles/sec (timer interval 0.10-0.16s). The reflex of using `Timer.publish(every: 0.32, ...)` (3 fps) — visible in the original Pacman/Frogger ContentViews — produces sluggish, glitchy gameplay where the player feels delays between intent and action. Web reference apps use requestAnimationFrame at 60fps with a separate game-step counter, which feels instant. macOS has no equivalent of RAF for SwiftUI, but Timer.publish at 0.13s (≈7.7 fps) is the practical sweet spot — fast enough to feel responsive, slow enough that the engine doesn't burn CPU. For continuous-motion games (Asteroids, Pong) use 1.0/60 = 0.0166s and run physics at full 60fps.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 after user feedback 'Pacman rendering is very slow and glitchy.' Root cause: original ContentView used 0.32s tick (3 fps). Web Pacman uses RAF 60fps + tile-step every ~150ms. Fixed by lowering Timer interval to 0.13s. Generalizes to ALL tile-grid games — Frogger had the same issue (0.55s = 1.8fps). The macOS reflex of 'use a slow timer to keep CPU low' produces an experience markedly worse than the same game on web.
examples: RIGHT (tile-grid game): private let timer = Timer.publish(every: 0.13, on: .main, in: .common).autoconnect() — ~7.7 tiles/sec, responsive.
RIGHT (continuous-motion game): private let timer = Timer.publish(every: 1.0/60.0, on: .main, in: .common).autoconnect() — 60fps physics.
WRONG: Timer.publish(every: 0.32, ...) in Pacman/Frogger — 3 fps tile stepping, feels glitchy.
WRONG: Timer.publish(every: 0.55, ...) in Frogger — 1.8 fps, traffic stutters.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid games where the player has continuous motion (Pacman, Snake, Bomberman) MUST use a direction-QUEUE pattern, not direct-on-keypress movement. Each arrow key press sets a `pendingDirection: GridDirection?` state variable. On every game tick, the engine reads pendingDirection and pivots the hero IF that direction is walkable, otherwise continues in the previous direction (`lastHeroDirection`). Single press persists across many tiles — the player doesn't have to tap-spam. This is the canonical web-Pacman pattern. Direct-on-keypress (call `engine.moveHero(direction)` from the keyboard handler) requires one press per tile of motion — feels broken and is what users will complain about as 'have to single step the arrow keys.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v3 feedback. User said 'I have to single step the arrow keys rather than having it be able to hold the key down.' Hold-to-repeat (phases [.down, .repeat]) is one solution but feels like Asteroids steering, not Pacman pursuit. The pendingDirection queue is what the original arcade Pacman + every web port use — single press = hero pivots when possible, continues otherwise. The pattern decouples 'player intent' from 'engine timing.'
examples: RIGHT: @State var pendingDirection: GridDirection? = nil; @State var lastHeroDirection: GridDirection = .left; key press → pendingDirection = direction; on tick → if isWalkable(pendingDirection) { move + clear pendingDirection } else if isWalkable(lastHeroDirection) { move }
RIGHT: queue persists until the move actually fires — player can press 'up' before reaching the intersection and Pacman will turn there automatically.
WRONG: key press → engine.moveHero(direction) directly. One key per tile of motion. Feels like single-stepping a debugger.
WRONG: phases [.down, .repeat] hold-to-move on a tile-grid pursuit game — produces Asteroids-style continuous input which doesn't fit the maze-pivot model.

guidelines.rule: Before designing a macOS reference app's gameplay or rendering from scratch, CHECK `harness/templates/miniapps/` for an existing web template of the same genre. Working production templates exist for arcade-pacman-v1, arcade-frogger, arcade-alien-abduction, board-checkers-mono, chess-game-board, connect-four-board-game, match-three-puzzle-game, simple-calculator-ui, texas-holdem-poker, word-puzzle-game, and more. These templates are HAND-CURATED FROM PRODUCTION HUB URLS — they encode hundreds of tuning decisions (lane count, speed multipliers, vehicle types, color palettes, key handling) that took real users + iteration to get right. Read the web template's render loop, key handlers, and visual setup BEFORE writing the macOS equivalent. The web Frogger has 5 traffic lanes with truck/sedan/sports variants; if the macOS Frogger uses 2 lanes of generic vehicles, that's not a creative choice — that's the engine being underspecified relative to a known-working reference.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 after user said: 'It seems a little crazy to me that the web versions of these games look and feel so much better than the macOS versions. ... This seems very ground hog day for me, didn't we work on this before?' Real incident: the macOS Frogger has 2 traffic lanes + 1 log row, but a 1456-line working web Frogger template existed in `harness/templates/miniapps/arcade-frogger/reference.html` with 5 lanes + 3 log lanes + truck/sedan/sports vehicle variants. I built the macOS version against the underspecified engine instead of consulting the web reference. Same applies to Pacman (2029-line web template existed). Wisdom rule's purpose: the next time someone (me or a generator) is about to build/polish a macOS game, point them at the web template first.
examples: RIGHT: about to polish macOS Frogger? `ls harness/templates/miniapps/arcade-frogger/` — read reference.html for lane count, vehicle types, log lengths. Match those structurally.
RIGHT: building Snake from scratch? Check if `harness/templates/miniapps/arcade-snake-*/` exists — if yes, mirror its render loop + apple-spawn rules.

WRONG: 'macOS Frogger should have 2 lanes of generic vehicles' — invented from the engine's stub map (`froggerLaneMap()`), not validated against the working web template that has 5 lanes + 3 vehicle types.

guidelines.rule: Game enemies / vehicles / units MUST be rendered with VECTOR SPRITES (Path/Canvas drawing with curves + multi-color fill), NOT as colored circles or emoji glyphs. A circle with a letter inside reads as 'placeholder graphic.' A multi-shape SVG-style sprite reads as a real game piece. The web codebase has `frameworks/web-components/resources/VectorSprites.js` with canvas-path drawDragon/drawSpaceship/drawCar functions — these patterns translate directly to SwiftUI Path + GraphicsContext.fill (Canvas view). For tower-defense enemies in particular: a 'normal' enemy circle next to a 'heavy' enemy circle next to a 'fast' enemy circle reads as three colored dots; the same enemies as articulated walker / tank / sprinter sprites read as different threats. Threat-level diversity (game-enemy-diversity-by-kind-table) is necessary but not sufficient — VISUAL distinction is the other half.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from user feedback 'Tower defense needs more turrets visual definition and the enemies need to be more than blobs.' Already filed game-boss-color-distinct-from-regular about color hierarchy, but that doesn't help if the base shape is a circle. The web codebase already solved this (VectorSprites.js); the macOS catalog hasn't yet. Until a Swift VectorSprites Lego exists, individual reference apps SHOULD draw distinct multi-shape sprites inline (per drawAsteroidsEnemy patterns in arcade.asteroids). When the Swift Lego ships, refactor to it.
examples: RIGHT: drawTankEnemy(at: point, color: .red) renders body rect + turret rect + tracks rect — recognizable as a tank silhouette.
RIGHT: Asteroids hunter = arrowhead pointed at player (multi-line stroke) — distinct from saucer's oval+dome (multi-fill).
WRONG: Circle().fill(.red) + Text('T') for 'tank' enemy. Reads as a colored dot with a letter, not a tank.
WRONG: emoji glyph inside a circle ("\u{1F47D}" for ghost). Emoji inherit system font rendering; they look like UI chrome, not game pieces.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid games with moving actors (Pacman, Frogger, Snake, dungeon crawlers, sokoban-with-enemies) MUST render via a SINGLE Canvas view inside a TimelineView(.animation), NOT via LazyVGrid with per-cell SwiftUI child views. The LazyVGrid pattern (one View per cell, each reading engine.map[point] + actors at point) rebuilds dozens or hundreds of child views every time the engine state changes — SwiftUI's diffing flashes/transitions cells in and out, producing a strobe-flicker effect that looks broken. The TimelineView+Canvas pattern paints the entire playfield in ONE draw pass at 60fps, and the engine's discrete tile-step state is INTERPOLATED visually: snapshot each actor's previousTile before engine.update, then in the Canvas closure compute `t = elapsed / tickInterval` (clamped 0..1) and draw at `lerp(previousTile, currentTile, t)`. The result: smooth continuous motion between tile-steps, no flicker, true 60fps render even though the game logic ticks at 5-8 Hz.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v3→v4. v3 had the tick rate fix (0.32s → 0.13s) but still looked seizure-inducing because every tick the LazyVGrid rebuilt 17×13 = 221 cells, each with its own actor child views, and SwiftUI's diff-driven re-render produced visible flicker on every cell that changed contents. User: 'now is just fast, the game play should be challenging but smooth from one frame to the next so it doesn't look like a blinking flashing seizure inducing game.' v4 replaces the entire grid with a single Canvas that paints walls + pellets + actors in one pass, 60fps via TimelineView, and interpolates actor positions. Smooth even at slow logic tick.
examples: RIGHT (architecture): TimelineView(.animation(minimumInterval: 1/60)) { timeline in Canvas { context, size in let t = (timeline.date.timeIntervalSince(lastTickTime) / tickInterval).clamped(0...1); drawMaze(context); drawActors(context, t: t) } }

RIGHT (snapshot): on each engine tick, capture `var snapshot: [UUID: TilePoint] = [:]; for actor in engine.actors { snapshot[actor.id] = actor.position }; previousActorPositions = snapshot; lastTickTime = .now` BEFORE engine.update().
RIGHT (interpolated draw): center = lerp(previousCenter, currentCenter, t) — actor visuals slide smoothly between cells.

WRONG: LazyVGrid(columns: ...) { ForEach(engine.map.allPoints()) { point in cell(point) } } where cell(point) reads engine.map[point] + actors at point. Every tick re-instantiates dozens of cell views; SwiftUI flickers them.

WRONG: per-actor view in a ZStack with .position(...) tied to engine.actor.position only — actor teleports tile-to-tile because there's no fractional interpolation between ticks. Looks like a strobe at any tick rate fast enough to be playable.

guidelines.rule: Game engines that auto-respawn entities (heroes after death, ghosts after eat, vehicles after exit) MUST persist a SPAWN ANCHOR set by the factory method, not pick respawn locations from heuristics like '(rows-1, columns/2)' or 'first walkable floor in row-major order.' Heuristics break silently when the map's bottom-middle is a wall (Pacman arcade layout) — respawn falls through to allPoints().first { .floor } which is the top-left interior, producing a 'hyperspace teleport' bug. Pattern: factory builds the engine, captures heroStart in a `private(set) var heroSpawn: PuzzlePoint?`, respawnHero() prefers heroSpawn first, then falls back to a heuristic, then a last-resort walkable-cell scan. Same applies to ghost pen positions (deadGhostContactTiles + ghostPenTile API), enemy spawn waves, etc.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v5 hyperspace bug. respawnHero() in GridAdventureEngine had `preferred = (rows-1, columns/2)` — but Pacman's arcade map has the entire bottom row as wall. The heuristic fell through to the row-major fallback which landed on (1,1) — top-left interior. User reported teleport-on-collision; took 1 session to find, would have been zero with an explicit anchor. Generalizes to any game engine with respawn semantics on a non-trivial map.
examples: RIGHT: GridAdventureEngine has `private(set) var heroSpawn: PuzzlePoint?` set in init() from `actors.first { $0.kind == .hero }?.position`. respawnHero() checks heroSpawn FIRST, only falls through to heuristics if nil.
RIGHT (ghost variant): deadGhostContactTiles records WHERE the ghost was eaten (as opposed to inferring from previousActorPositions). View uses contact-tile as the float-home animation source.
WRONG: respawnHero uses `(rows-1, columns/2)` as the 'preferred' point. Maps with solid bottom rows fail silently. User experiences a teleport bug.

guidelines.rule: When a game engine resolves a transient event (collision, eat, kill), the engine should expose a flag (`deferRespawn: Bool`, `deferEnemyRespawn: Bool`) that lets the View OPT INTO controlling the animation timing. With `defer*=true`: the engine still updates discrete state (decrements lives, marks ghost dead, awards score) but DOES NOT teleport entities away from the contact tile. The View runs whatever animation it wants (death-shrink, eyes-float-home, materialize-in-pen) and then calls `engine.respawnHeroToStart()` / `engine.respawnEatenGhost(id:)` to commit the position change. Without this flag, the engine teleports the entity instantly — and the View can't draw a death animation at the contact tile because the engine already moved it. (User-perceived as 'I died from a tile away' or 'ghost teleports to pen with no transition.')
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v7 + v9 bugs. v7: ghost collision → engine.loseLife → respawnHero teleports Pac to spawn → View tries to animate death-shrink at previousActorPositions[hero] (PRE-tick) which was 1 tile away from the actual contact. v9: same problem for ghost-eaten — engine teleported eaten ghost to pen instantly, View animation lerped from previousActorPositions which was the ghost's pre-tick tile, not the contact tile. Both fixed by adding defer flags + explicit contact-tile fields (deadGhostContactTiles) to the engine. Generalizes to ANY engine with auto-respawn that wants to support a richer animation layer.
examples: RIGHT (engine API): `public var deferRespawn: Bool = false` (opt-in); on collision, if deferRespawn=true, leave entity on contact tile + flag dead + record contact tile. Public `respawnHeroToStart()` / `respawnEatenGhost(id:)` for View-controlled commit.
RIGHT (View pattern): on detecting lives drop, capture death-start time → run animation (shrink, eyes-float, materialize) → at end of pause, call engine.respawnX() to commit teleport.
WRONG: View uses previousActorPositions to guess where the contact happened. Pre-tick position is one step earlier than contact tile, so the animation freezes in the wrong place.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid games where the engine eats items on-tile (Pacman pellets, Bomberman explosions clearing crates) should keep the just-eaten item RENDERED AT FADING OPACITY for one tick interval — otherwise the visual lerp lags the engine state and items vanish 'from a tile away.' Pattern: View snapshots the set of items present BEFORE engine.update; after engine.update, diffs against the new set; items in the diff enter a `fadingItems: [(point, kind, eatenAt)]` collection; the renderer draws them at decreasing opacity over the tick interval. When the player visually arrives at the tile (t=1), the item has faded to 0 alpha — the item disappears at the moment of contact, not at the moment of intent.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v8 fix. User reported 'I eat a pellet before I'm fully over it as well, so maybe the collision bubble around the pacman is too big?' Root cause: engine eats pellets on-tile (instant when Pac arrives via engine.moveHero), but the View's visual lerp lasts a full tickInterval — so the pellet vanished when Pac was visually still mid-lerp toward it. Fix: render eaten pellets at fading opacity over the tickInterval. Generalizes to any per-tile pickup/collectible with a moving collector.
examples: RIGHT: tick() snapshots `pelletPositionsBeforeTick`, runs engine.update, then diffs `before - after` to find newly-eaten pellets. Each enters `fadingPellets: [(point, kind, eatenAt: Date)]`. drawMaze renders them at alpha 1 - (now - eatenAt) / tickInterval.
RIGHT (multiple kinds): track power-pellet positions separately from regular pellets so the larger fading sprite renders correctly.
WRONG: pellet vanishes the instant engine.removes it. Player sees 'ate from a tile away.'

guidelines.rule: Entity respawn (after death, eaten, exhausted) in arcade games SHOULD play a 3-stage animation rather than instant teleport: (1) FLOAT-HOME (1.0–1.5s) — the dead entity lerps from contact tile → respawn tile, often as a stripped-down visual (eyes only for ghosts, ship outline for spaceships, soul for RPG creatures); (2) PEN-PAUSE (1.0–2.0s) — entity sits at respawn tile in stripped state, building anticipation; (3) MATERIALIZE (0.3–0.6s) — full body fades back in (alpha 0→1) at the respawn tile, signaling 'back in play.' Total cycle 2.5–4s. Without the multi-stage anim, the respawn feels like a glitch. Pair with engine.deferEntityRespawn flag so the engine doesn't teleport prematurely.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 after Pacman v9. Original v8 implementation was a single-stage 1.6s eyes-float; user reported 'no delay between the respawn the respawn should take at least 3 seconds and start back at the box. There should be an animation that goes into the box showing the ghost getting respawned.' v9 split into 3 stages totaling 3.5s. Generalizes to: spaceships exploding+drifting+rebuilding, RPG creatures dying+ghost-floating+resurrecting, etc.
examples: RIGHT: 3-stage animation (1.5s float + 1.5s pause + 0.5s materialize), View tracks startedAt per entity, draws stage-appropriate visuals based on (now - startedAt).
RIGHT: stripped-down visual during stages 1+2 (eyes only / wireframe / silhouette) signals 'not yet alive,' full body during stage 3 signals 're-entering play.'
WRONG: instant respawn — entity teleports back at full body, looks like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: Game speed/difficulty ramp should use TWO axes: an INTRA-LEVEL ramp (time on current level, e.g. 0–60s) AND an INTER-LEVEL boost (per LevelManager.currentLevel). The intra-level ramp gives the player time to acclimate at level start ('start slow, get faster'); the inter-level boost means each new level starts MEASURABLY harder than the previous level's start. Use clamps: a `tickIntervalFloor` so even L20 doesn't go below playable. Formula: `effective = max(floor, intraLevelInterp(start → endL1, t) - levelBoost(currentLevel))`. Without two axes, designers either ramp too fast within L1 (player overwhelmed) OR don't ramp at all (L5 feels like L1 with bigger numbers).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 after Pacman v6 → v10 iteration. User feedback at each step ('starts off at a great pace, but it needs to be a bit more gradual,' 'by the time I'm reaching the end of level 1, it is really going too fast for this just to be a level 1 game'). The two-axis model lets you tune L1 to feel comfortable while still making L5 frantic — without those being the same parameter.
examples: RIGHT: `currentTickInterval = max(floor, lerp(start, endL1, intraT) - min(maxBoost, (level-1) * 0.01))`. L1 ranges start→endL1 over levelSpeedRampSeconds. L5+ caps at floor.
RIGHT (Pacman v10): start=0.30s, endL1=0.24s, ramp=90s, perLevelBoost=0.01s capped at 0.05s, floor=0.13s. L1 = 3.3→4.2 tiles/sec; L5 = ~5.3 tiles/sec; L9+ caps at 7.7 tiles/sec.
WRONG: single-axis ramp — `tickInterval = max(floor, start - level * X)`. L1 doesn't ramp (instantly at the L1 cap), L5 jumps to frantic.
WRONG: only intra-level ramp — every level identical.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid games (Pacman, dungeon crawlers, Sokoban, Frogger, any maze/grid layout) MUST include a BFS connectivity smoke test that runs at app launch (or in unit tests). Test: from the hero spawn, BFS through all non-wall cells; assert `reachable.count == floor.count`. Layouts authored by hand WILL have unreachable cells — closed inner rings, stranded fruit tiles, walls that should have been gaps. Catching this at launch (rather than first-play user complaint) is a 30-line check that pays for itself the first time it fires. Also assert that all non-hero actors (ghosts, fruit, power pellets) are reachable from the hero spawn — these are the highest-priority isolation bugs.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 after shipping Pacman v10 with L3 having unbroken concentric inner rings — fruit + 53 cells unreachable. User noticed within 5 seconds of switching to L3. A 30-line BFS check would have caught this at build time. ArcadeSmokeTests.verifyPacmanLayoutsConnected() now exists; ExampleAppInit prints the report on every launch. Generalizes to any tile-grid layout shipped as code.
examples: RIGHT: ArcadeSmokeTests.verifyPacmanLayoutsConnected() iterates all maze factory methods, BFS from hero, asserts reachable.count == floor.count + every actor reachable. Called from ExampleAppInit.init().
RIGHT (output format): '[ArcadeSmoke] L3 OK: 107 floor cells all reachable' or '[ArcadeSmoke] L3 FAIL: 53 unreachable, isolated actors=[fruit@(6,8)]'.
WRONG: ship hand-authored layouts with no connectivity check. User finds the dead zones; you spend a session iterating.

guidelines.rule: Tile-grid game maps with multiple actor placements (hero, enemies, pickups, exits) should be authored as STRING ARRAYS with character codes for each actor type, then parsed by a single shared `buildEngine(layout:)` helper. Pacman pattern: `'#'` = wall, `'.'` = floor + pellet, `'o'` = power pellet, `'H'` = hero, `'G'` = ghost, `'F'` = fruit, `' '` = floor (no pellet). Adding a new map is just defining another 17×13 string array. Without this pattern, each map duplicates ~50 lines of 'place hero at X, place ghosts at Y, place pellets at every other cell, except Z' code — error-prone, hard to scan visually, hard to compare.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-05 from Pacman v10. The original pacmanArcadeMap was 50 lines of hardcoded point sets. Adding 4 more mazes that way would have been 200 lines of repetition. The character-code approach: 13 strings + 50 lines of buildPacmanEngine helper, and every new map is just a new string array. Made the L3-bug fix a 1-line edit.
examples: RIGHT: layout = ["#################", "#G.o.........o.G#", ...]; buildPacmanEngine(layout) parses chars in one pass.
RIGHT (visual review): looking at the string array, you can SEE the maze layout. Walls stand out, hero spawn is visible, ghost spawn corners are obvious.
WRONG: map factory has 'let heroStart = PuzzlePoint(row: 11, col: 8); let ghostStarts = [...]' followed by a long for-loop over allPoints() inserting walls. Hard to verify by eye.

guidelines.rule: When a recipe selects MANY components (e.g. recipe.spritekit-physics selects 7: arcade.spritekit-physics-helpers + 6 effects), only the recipe's `requiredNow` list MUST be composed by your app's Swift glue. Optional/decorative effects (animation-intensity, sound-engine, level-manager, high-score-manager, pop-burst, score-rise) get copied verbatim into Sources/<Target>/MChatAIComponents/ but you only need to reference them if you're actually using them in your gameplay. The Lego gate enforces composition for `requiredNow` strictly and accepts partial composition for the rest. Don't twist your gameplay shape to forcibly reference every selected component — pick the effects that make sense for the genre, ignore the rest.
guidelines.why: Marble Madness canary 2026-05-09 (composition-fix-marble) succeeded ONLY after the gate was relaxed to enforce composition just for `requiredNow`. Bubble Pop canary 2026-05-09 had earlier gate-rejected itself into a Lunar Lander golden assembly fallback because it referenced AnimationIntensity but not SoundEngine/LevelManager/etc. The gate's old policy turned a working partial-composition into a Frankenstein.
examples: RIGHT (BubblePop): use AnimationIntensity for tap-pop animation tuning. Skip SoundEngine if your game has no audio. Skip LevelManager if it's a single-screen game.
RIGHT (MarbleMadness): compose SKPhysicsHelpers + SKArcadeCategory (the physics-helpers component is requiredNow). Reach for effects only as needed.
WRONG: padding your code with `let _ = SoundEngine.shared` just to satisfy the composition check — this hides the optional-effect intent.

guidelines.rule: When using SwiftUI shape strokes/fills, ONLY reference real ShapeStyle members. Common LLM hallucinations: `.gold`, `.silver`, `.bronze`, `.crimson`, `.azure` — NONE of these exist on `Color` or `ShapeStyle`. Use the canonical members (`.red`, `.orange`, `.yellow`, `.green`, `.mint`, `.teal`, `.cyan`, `.blue`, `.indigo`, `.purple`, `.pink`, `.brown`, `.white`, `.gray`, `.black`, `.primary`, `.secondary`) or construct via `Color(red:green:blue:)` / `Color(.sRGB, ...)` / `Color(hex:)` with explicit values. For gold-flavored UI: `Color(red: 1.0, green: 0.84, blue: 0.0)` or just `.yellow`.
guidelines.why: Match3 canary 2026-05-09 (catalog-verify-match3) shipped with `Circle().stroke(.gold, lineWidth: ...)` — Swift error: `type 'ShapeStyle' has no member 'gold'`. Build failed despite the rest of the app being clean. This pattern recurs because LLMs over-generalize from system-color naming conventions in other frameworks (Apple watchOS, macOS Catalina design tokens, etc.) — none of which expose a `.gold` ShapeStyle. Always-construct-explicitly avoids the hallucination class entirely.
examples: RIGHT: Circle().stroke(.yellow, lineWidth: 4)
RIGHT: Circle().stroke(Color(red: 1.0, green: 0.84, blue: 0.0), lineWidth: 4)  // gold
RIGHT: .foregroundStyle(Color(red: 0.83, green: 0.69, blue: 0.22))  // antique-gold
WRONG: Circle().stroke(.gold, lineWidth: 4)  // 'ShapeStyle' has no member 'gold'
WRONG: .foregroundColor(.silver)  // 'Color' has no member 'silver'
WRONG: Text("...").foregroundStyle(.bronze)  // doesn't exist

guidelines.rule: ONLY reference symbols that come from components listed in the recipe's selection — DO NOT reach for `SoundEngine`, `AnimationIntensity`, `LevelManager`, `HighScoreManager`, `PopBurst`, `ScoreRise`, `BoardShake`, `SpawnDrop`, `HintPulse`, etc. unless the marker file `mchatai-macos-components-used.json` lists their component IDs (e.g. `effects.sound-engine`, `effects.animation-intensity`). Look at the marker BEFORE writing imports/usages. If the recipe didn't select an effect you want, EITHER (a) implement that capability inline (a few lines of plain Swift will compile), OR (b) propose a recipe change in your continueWizard turn — but never reference a component that wasn't shipped by the matcher. Compile errors of the form `cannot find 'SoundEngine' in scope` mean you reached for an unselected Lego.
guidelines.why: Pong canary 2026-05-09 (catalog-verify-pong) shipped with `@ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared` and `@StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(...)` even though recipe.arcade-pong's marker only listed arcade.core/arcade.pong/arcade.smoke-tests/effects.pop-burst/effects.score-rise/effects.board-shake. SoundEngine.swift / AnimationIntensity.swift / LevelManager.swift were never copied → swift build failed with `cannot find 'SoundEngine' in scope`. This is the inverse of mac-031: there we said 'don't pad code to compose every selected component'; here we say 'don't reach for components that weren't selected at all'.
examples: RIGHT: read mchatai-macos-components-used.json first; if it lists `effects.sound-engine`, use SoundEngine.shared. If it doesn't, write `NSSound.beep()` or skip audio.
RIGHT (audio without SoundEngine selected): `NSSound(named: "Pop")?.play()` or `import AudioToolbox; AudioServicesPlaySystemSound(1057)` — both work without any component.
WRONG: `@ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared` when effects.sound-engine isn't in the marker.
WRONG: `private let intensity: AnimationIntensity = .standard` when effects.animation-intensity isn't in the marker.

guidelines.rule: When your recipe selects ANY SwiftUI effect Lego (effects.pop-burst, effects.score-rise, effects.board-shake, effects.spawn-drop, effects.hint-pulse), Package.swift MUST declare BOTH (a) `// swift-tools-version: 6.2` (or higher) AND (b) `.macOS(.v26)` (or higher). The platform `.v26` was introduced in PackageDescription 6.2 — using `swift-tools-version: 6.0` with `.macOS(.v26)` produces error: `'v26' is unavailable`. All effect Legos are annotated `@available(macOS 26.0, *)` because they use SwiftUI 5.0 / iOS 18 / macOS Sequoia animation APIs (Animatable, ScaledMetric improvements, KeyframeAnimator). If your Package.swift defaults to `.macOS(.v12)` (Claude's habit) the build fails with `'popBurst(trigger:intensity:color:)' is only available in macOS 26.0 or newer`. ALWAYS use `swift-tools-version: 6.2` AND `.macOS(.v26)` for any recipe with SwiftUI-effect components.
guidelines.why: Pong canary 2026-05-09 (catalog-verify-pong refire) shipped Package.swift with `.macOS(.v12)` but ContentView.swift used `.popBurst(trigger:...)`, `.scoreRise(...)`, `.boardShake(...)` — 4 errors all of form `only available in macOS 26.0 or newer`. The Lego components themselves are correctly annotated. The bug is the mismatch between the wizard-generated Package.swift platform target and the Lego availability requirements. Final pong validation 2026-05-09 used .macOS(.v26) with swift-tools 6.0 → error: `'v26' is unavailable` from Swift PackageDescription 6.2 requirement.
examples: RIGHT: Package.swift starts `// swift-tools-version: 6.2` AND `platforms: [.macOS(.v26)]`
RIGHT: `// swift-tools-version: 6.0` AND `platforms: [.macOS(.v15)]` for non-effect recipes
WRONG: `// swift-tools-version: 6.0` with `.macOS(.v26)` → error: `'v26' is unavailable` (v26 requires 6.2)
WRONG: `// swift-tools-version: 6.2` with `.macOS(.v12)` → effect modifier calls fail with `'popBurst' is only available in macOS 26.0 or newer`

guidelines.rule: Lego types expose ONLY the surface that's documented in their `exports` array in `_index.json` and the `BEGIN ... END` block in their .swift file. Don't assume other properties or nested types exist. Common Claude-hallucination examples that DO NOT work: `paddle.halfWidth` / `paddle.halfHeight` (these are STATIC class constants on PongEngine: `PongEngine.paddleHalfWidth`, NOT instance properties on ArcadeBody); `PongEngine.Event` (no nested type — use the public enum names from PongEngine.swift or read the file); `ArcadeBody.size` / `ArcadeBody.frame` (only `radius` exists). When you need a property and aren't 100% sure it's there, ALWAYS read the canonical .swift file under `MChatAIComponents/` first.
guidelines.why: Pong canary 2026-05-09 (catalog-verify-pong refire) shipped with paddle.halfWidth / paddle.halfHeight (4 errors — paddles are ArcadeBody instances, halfWidth/halfHeight are PongEngine class constants) and `PongEngine.Event` (1 error — no such nested type exists). LLMs over-generalize from common game-dev API conventions; the canonical source is the only ground truth.
examples: RIGHT: `let halfW = PongEngine.paddleHalfWidth` (static access to class constant).
RIGHT: read `Sources/<Target>/MChatAIComponents/PongEngine.swift` first to learn the surface.
WRONG: `paddle.halfHeight` where `paddle: ArcadeBody` — ArcadeBody only has position/velocity/radius/angle/isAlive.
WRONG: `PongEngine.Event.scored` — PongEngine has no Event type; check the file first.

guidelines.rule: macos-components is a SOURCE CATALOG, NOT a SwiftPM package. Components are COPIED VERBATIM into `Sources/<Target>/MChatAIComponents/` — they ARE NOT imported as `.product(name:..., package:'macos-components')` and they ARE NOT declared as `.package(url: 'https://github.com/mchatai/macos-components', ...)` (that URL doesn't exist as a SwiftPM-shaped repo). DO NOT add a `dependencies:` array referring to macos-components. The Package.swift for a Lego-using app should have an EMPTY `dependencies: []` (or omit the field entirely). The marker file `mchatai-macos-components-used.json` is the only declaration needed; the verbatim copies in `MChatAIComponents/` are how the app gets the source. They compile as part of YOUR executable target. ALSO: do NOT write `import PongEngine` / `import ArcadeCore` / `import GridPuzzleCore` / `import SpriteKitPhysicsHelpers` / etc. The components compile inline as part of YOUR target — they are NOT separate modules. The only `import` statements you need are system frameworks (SwiftUI, AppKit, SpriteKit, AudioToolbox, Foundation).
guidelines.why: Final pong validation 2026-05-09 (final-pong-validate-2026-05-09-1224) had Package.swift with `.package(url: 'https://github.com/mchatai/macos-components', branch: 'main')` and `.product(name: 'PongEngine', package: 'macos-components')` — swift build failed with 'Failed to clone repository https://github.com/mchatai/macos-components' (the URL doesn't exist as a SwiftPM-shaped repo with Package.swift exposing PongEngine product). The PongEngine.swift was correctly copied verbatim into Sources/PongArcade/MChatAIComponents/ — it should compile as part of the PongArcade target. Adding the package dependency is a fundamental misunderstanding of the catalog architecture.
examples:
RIGHT (Package.swift for any Lego-using app):
```
let package = Package(
    name: "PongArcade",
    platforms: [.macOS(.v26)],
    products: [.executable(name: "PongArcade", targets: ["PongArcade"])],
    targets: [
        .executableTarget(name: "PongArcade", path: "Sources/PongArcade")
    ]
)
```
No dependencies. Components are inside Sources/PongArcade/MChatAIComponents/.
WRONG: `.package(url: 'https://github.com/mchatai/macos-components', branch: 'main')` — that's not how the catalog works.
WRONG: `.product(name: 'PongEngine', package: 'macos-components')` — components compile as part of YOUR target.
WRONG: `import PongEngine` (compiles inline, not a module).
WRONG: `import ArcadeCore` (same — verbatim source copy in MChatAIComponents/, not a separate module).
RIGHT: `import SwiftUI` and `import SpriteKit` — only system frameworks need imports.

guidelines.rule: Swift target/module names MUST be valid Swift identifiers: [A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*. Do NOT use parentheses, slashes, hyphens, spaces, or other punctuation in target names OR in file names that contain `@main`. Common Claude-hallucination examples: `PongArcade(macOS)App.swift`, `Bubble-Pop-App.swift`, `My App.swift`. The parens/dashes/spaces break SwiftPM compilation and Xcode resolution. Use `PongArcadeApp.swift`, `BubblePopApp.swift`, `MyApp.swift`. Same for the Package.swift `name:` field — keep it alphanumeric.
guidelines.why: Final pong validation 2026-05-09 had `Sources/PongArcade(macOS)App.swift` with `@main` — the parens broke macro expansion. Multiple historical canaries have shipped target names with parens (when LLM picks up the recipe.name like `Native (macOS) Pong` and tries to slug it). Always-alphanumeric avoids the class.
examples: RIGHT: `Sources/PongArcade/PongArcadeApp.swift` with `@main struct PongArcadeApp { ... }`.
WRONG: `Sources/PongArcade(macOS)App.swift` — parens not valid in identifier.
WRONG: `Sources/Bubble-Pop-App.swift` — dashes break.
WRONG: `Package(name: "Pong (Arcade)")` — parens in target name break SwiftPM resolution.
